title: Anesthesia for Respiratory Diseases MCQ – Chapter 19
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: Which two factors are the most important predictors of postoperative pulmonary complications?

options.B: Dyspnea at minimal activity and site of surgery
options.C: Heavy smoking and congestive heart failure
options.D: Serum albumin < 3.5 g/dL and severity of pulmonary disease

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'Dyspnea at minimal activity and site of surgery are the two most important predictors of postoperative complications.'

questions.question: What is the ideal duration for stopping smoking before surgery?
questions.explanation: The text states 'Ideally smoking should be stopped 8 weeks before surgery however stopping smoking 12 hours before may be beneficial by reducing carboxyhemoglobin levels.'

questions.question: Stopping smoking 12 hours before surgery is beneficial primarily because it reduces:
questions.explanation: The text states that stopping smoking 12 hours before surgery 'may be beneficial by reducing carboxyhemoglobin levels.'

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a major risk factor for postoperative pulmonary complications?
questions.explanation: The listed risk factors include age > 60, severity of pulmonary disease, ASA grade > 2, CHF, heavy smoking, surgery under GA, surgical factors, and serum albumin < 3.5 g/dL. BMI > 30 is not mentioned.

questions.question: What surgical duration is cited as a risk factor for postoperative pulmonary complications?
questions.explanation: The text lists surgical factors including 'duration (> 3 hours)' as a major risk factor.

questions.question: Regarding pulmonary function tests (PFT) in patients with pulmonary disease, the text states that:

options.A: PFT should be done routinely in all patients
options.B: PFT determines severity but routine use is not recommended
options.C: PFT is the best predictor of postoperative complications
options.D: PFT has no role in assessing pulmonary disease

questions.explanation: The text states 'Although pulmonary function test (PFT) do determine the severity of disease but are not always predictors of complications therefore routine use of PFT is not recommended.'

questions.question: Why should premedication with H2 blockers (Ranitidine) be avoided in patients with pulmonary disease?

options.B: H2 blockade leads to unopposed H1 activation and bronchospasm

questions.explanation: The text states 'Premedication with H2 blockers (Ranitidine) should be avoided because H2 blockade leads to unopposed H1 activation and bronchospasm.'

questions.question: Why is premedication with benzodiazepines avoided in patients with pulmonary disease?

options.C: These patients are more sensitive to respiratory depression

questions.explanation: The text states 'Premedication with benzodiazepines should be avoided as these patients are more sensitive to respiratory depression.'

questions.question: Regarding anticholinergic premedication in pulmonary disease patients, the text states:

options.A: It is contraindicated due to excessive drying of secretions
options.B: It is theoretically beneficial by causing bronchodilatation but clinical advantage has not been seen
options.C: It is the premedication of choice
options.D: It should always be given with bronchodilators

questions.explanation: The text states 'Theoretically premedication with anticholinergic is beneficial by causing bronchodilatation, however, clinical advantage has not been seen.'

questions.question: What is the role of prophylactic nebulization in an asymptomatic patient with pulmonary disease?

options.A: It is the standard of care preoperatively
options.B: It should be given 30 minutes before surgery
options.C: There is no role of prophylactic nebulization in asymptomatic patients
options.D: It is recommended only for COPD patients

questions.explanation: The text clearly states 'there is no role of prophylactic nebulization in asymptomatic patient.'

questions.question: For patients with pulmonary disease, which type of anesthesia is always preferred?
questions.explanation: The text states 'Regional anesthesia is always preferred over general anesthesia.'
questions.reference: Ch 19, Section: Choice of Anesthesia, Page 192

questions.question: Central neuraxial block above which level can cause significant distress in COPD patients?
questions.explanation: The text states central neuraxial blocks are excellent if below T6. Level above T6 can cause paralysis of abdominal muscles causing significant distress, particularly in COPD patients.
questions.reference: Ch 19, Section: Choice of Anesthesia, Page 192-193

questions.question: Why does a neuraxial block above T6 cause particular distress in COPD patients?

options.B: It leads to bronchospasm from parasympathetic stimulation
options.C: These patients have active expiration dependent on abdominal muscles which get paralyzed

questions.explanation: The text explains that block above T6 'can cause paralysis of abdominal muscles causing significant distress (particularly in COPD patients) because these patients have active expiration dependent on abdominal muscles.'
questions.reference: Ch 19, Section: Choice of Anesthesia, Page 192-193

questions.question: Dry gases during general anesthesia can cause atelectasis by which mechanism?
questions.explanation: The text states 'Dry gases can inhibit ciliary function causing atelectasis in postoperative period.'

questions.question: Which is the ONLY inhalational agent that does NOT inhibit ciliary activity?
questions.explanation: The text states 'all inhalational agents (except ether) also inhibit ciliary activity.'

questions.question: What is the recommended tidal volume for ventilating patients with pulmonary disease to avoid barotrauma?
questions.explanation: The text states 'Ventilation principle includes low tidal volume (6–8 mL/kg) to avoid barotrauma.'

questions.question: What respiratory rate is recommended for ventilating patients with pulmonary disease?
questions.explanation: The text states a 'slow respiratory rate of (6–10 breaths/min.) to allow adequate exhalation and prevent air trapping.'

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT part of the ventilation principles for pulmonary disease patients?
questions.explanation: The ventilation principles include low tidal volume, low I:E ratio, and slow respiratory rate (6–10 breaths/min). A high respiratory rate would prevent adequate exhalation and cause air trapping.

questions.question: Improvement in pulmonary functions postoperatively should ideally be guided by:
questions.explanation: The text states 'Ideally the improvement in pulmonary functions should be guided by functional residual capacity (FRC).'

questions.question: What is the most preferred approach for pain management in thoracic and upper abdominal surgeries in respiratory compromised patients?
questions.explanation: The text states 'Thoracic epidural is the most preferred, most safe, most effective and most commonly used approach to achieve analgesia for thoracic and upper abdominal surgeries.'

questions.question: Why is cryoanalgesia not commonly used for postoperative pain relief despite producing effective pain relief?

options.A: Risk of pneumothorax and nerve damage
options.B: Delayed onset, nonavailability of probes, and very long-lasting effect (nerve recovery 1–6 months)

questions.explanation: The text states cryoanalgesia is 'not used commonly due to delayed onset, nonavailability of probes and very long lasting effect (nerve recovery may even take 1–6 months).'

questions.question: Why should parenteral opioids be avoided in respiratory compromised patients for postoperative pain management?
questions.explanation: The text states 'Parenteral opioids can cause respiratory depression therefore should be avoided as far as possible.'

questions.question: In local infiltration for thoracotomy pain, how many levels above and below the incision site should be infiltrated?
questions.explanation: The text mentions 'local infiltration (2 levels above and below the thoracotomy incision site)' as one of the analgesic techniques.

questions.question: For elective surgery in an asthma patient, what percentage of predicted peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) should ideally be achieved?
questions.explanation: The text states the patient 'ideally should have 80% of predicted value of peak expiratory flow rate.'

questions.question: Why is ketamine generally avoided in asthma patients despite being the best bronchodilator?

options.B: Its side effects (especially vivid reactions) and propensity to increase tracheobronchial secretions
options.D: It has no IV formulation available

questions.explanation: The text states 'Ketamine in spite of its best bronchodilator property is generally avoided due to its side effect (especially vivid reactions) and propensity to increase tracheobronchial secretions.'

questions.question: In which specific clinical scenario is the use of ketamine indicated in asthma patients?

options.A: All elective surgeries in asthma patients
options.B: Patients in active asthma (wheezing) undergoing life-threatening emergency surgeries

questions.explanation: The text states 'The use of ketamine in present day practice is restricted to patients in active asthma (wheezing) undergoing life threatening emergency surgeries.'

questions.question: Ketamine in a patient taking theophylline can precipitate:
questions.explanation: The text states 'Ketamine in patient taking theophylline can precipitate seizures.'

questions.question: Which induction agent is most preferred for asthma patients in present-day anesthesia practice?
questions.explanation: The text states 'Propofol is a reasonable bronchodilator devoid of side effects seen with ketamine therefore, is more preferred for asthma patients in present day anesthesia practice.'

questions.question: Which induction agent can induce bronchoconstriction and should be avoided in asthma patients?
questions.explanation: The text states 'Thiopentone can induce bronchoconstriction therefore, should be avoided.'

questions.question: What is the most important consideration during induction in an asthma patient?

options.B: Preventing reflex stimulation of airways by laryngoscopy and intubation

questions.explanation: The text states 'The most important consideration is to prevent reflex stimulation of airways by laryngoscopy and intubation.'

questions.question: Which inhalational agent is the agent of choice for maintenance in asthma patients?
questions.explanation: The text states 'Sevoflurane is the inhalational agent of choice for asthma patients.'

questions.question: Why is halothane not preferred over sevoflurane for asthma despite producing slightly more bronchodilatation?

options.B: It causes increased possibility of arrhythmias

questions.explanation: The text states 'Halothane, in spite of producing little more bronchodilatation than sevoflurane in asthma patients is not preferred because of the increase possibility of arrhythmias.'

questions.question: Which class of muscle relaxant is preferred in asthma patients and why?

options.B: Steroidal class — benzylisoquinolines release histamine causing bronchospasm
options.C: Depolarizing agents — they have the shortest duration
options.D: Ester-based relaxants — they are metabolized fastest

questions.explanation: The text states 'Steroidal class of muscle relaxant is preferred. Benzylisoquinolines by release histamine can precipitate bronchospasm.'
questions.reference: Ch 19, Section: Asthma – Muscle Relaxant, Page 193

questions.question: To avoid immediate postoperative bronchospasm in asthma patients, extubation should be done:

options.A: When the patient is fully awake
options.B: When the patient is deep (deep extubation)

questions.explanation: The text states 'To avoid immediate postoperative bronchospasm extubation should be done when patient is deep (deep extubation).'

questions.question: What is the FIRST step before starting treatment for suspected intraoperative bronchospasm?

options.B: Increase depth of anesthesia with sevoflurane
options.C: Rule out other causes like ETT obstruction, kinking, inadequate depth, pulmonary edema, aspiration, embolus, or pneumothorax

questions.explanation: Step 1: Before starting therapy for asthma it is must to rule out other causes of increased airway pressure/bronchospasm.
questions.reference: Ch 19, Section: Management of Intraoperative Bronchospasm, Page 194

questions.question: Once confirmed that intraoperative bronchospasm is due to asthma, what is the first line of treatment?

options.C: Increasing the depth of anesthesia with inhalational agent (preferably sevoflurane)

questions.explanation: Step 2: It should first be treated by increasing the depth of anesthesia with inhalational agent (preferably sevoflurane).
questions.reference: Ch 19, Section: Management of Intraoperative Bronchospasm, Page 194

questions.question: For refractory bronchospasm not responding to inhaled beta-2 agonists and IV steroids, which agents should be considered?

options.A: IV magnesium and calcium channel blockers
options.D: Leukotriene antagonists and mast cell stabilizers

questions.explanation: Step 5: For refractory bronchospasm consider epinephrine, ketamine or aminophylline infusion.
questions.reference: Ch 19, Section: Management of Intraoperative Bronchospasm, Page 194

questions.question: Which is the most common pulmonary disease encountered in clinical practice?
questions.explanation: The text states 'Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is the most common pulmonary disease encountered in clinical practice.'

questions.question: Why should nitrous oxide be avoided in emphysema patients?

options.B: It can cause expansion of bullae leading to rupture and pneumothorax

questions.explanation: The text states 'Nitrous oxide should not be used in emphysema. It can cause expansion of bullae leading to rupture and pneumothorax.'

questions.question: In COPD patients with evidence of pulmonary hypertension (cor pulmonale), which agent should be avoided?
questions.explanation: The text states 'Cor pulmonale must be ruled out and if there is any evidence of pulmonary hypertension then nitrous oxide should be avoided.'

questions.question: The anesthetic management of COPD patients is described as similar to which group of patients with additional considerations?
questions.explanation: The text states 'The anesthetic management of COPD patients is similar to asthma patient with additional emphasis on the following points.'

questions.question: Which of the following measures is NOT mentioned as part of preoperative stabilization of COPD patients?

options.D: Correcting spasm and improving pulmonary condition by physiotherapy

questions.explanation: The text mentions stopping smoking, treating infection, correcting spasm (asthmatic bronchitis), and improve pulmonary condition by physiotherapy, but not long-term oxygen therapy.

questions.question: Patients with restrictive lung disease are more prone to hypoxemia during induction because of:
questions.explanation: The text states 'These patients have low FRC therefore are more prone for hypoxemia during induction.'
questions.reference: Ch 19, Section: Restrictive Lung Diseases, Page 194

questions.question: What is the most important principle of ventilatory management in restrictive lung disease during general anesthesia?

options.C: Avoiding large tidal volumes and inspiratory pressures

questions.explanation: The text states 'avoiding large tidal volumes and inspiratory pressures is the most important principle of ventilatory management during general anesthesia.'
questions.reference: Ch 19, Section: Restrictive Lung Diseases, Page 194

questions.question: Regarding tuberculosis patients, antitubercular drugs should be continued but assessment of which organ function is a must?
questions.explanation: The text states 'Antitubercular drugs should be continued however, assessment of liver functions is must.'

questions.question: In a patient with active TB undergoing emergency surgery, which equipment should be discarded after the case?

options.C: All equipment including the anesthesia machine

questions.explanation: The text states 'Equipment coming in direct contact with patient like mask, laryngeal mask airway (LMA), breathing circuits should be discarded after case while other equipment and theater should be sterilized properly.'

questions.question: For how long should elective surgery be deferred in a patient with significant URTI?
questions.explanation: The text states surgery should 'ideally be deferred for 6 weeks' as 'airway remains hyperactive for 5–6 weeks after a significant URTI.'
questions.reference: Ch 19, Section: Respiratory Tract Infection, Page 195

questions.question: A patient with URTI undergoing surgery has how many times increased risk of bronchospasm compared to a healthy patient?
questions.explanation: The text states patients with active URTI are prone to develop 'bronchospasm (10 times)' compared to normal.
questions.reference: Ch 19, Section: Respiratory Tract Infection, Page 195

questions.question: In a patient with active URTI requiring general anesthesia, which airway device is preferred over endotracheal intubation?
questions.explanation: The text states 'If GA is to be given then prefer to get the surgery done under laryngeal mask airway (LMA). Avoid intubation as far as possible.'
questions.reference: Ch 19, Section: Respiratory Tract Infection, Page 195

questions.question: What FEV1 value is one of the operative criteria indicating need for thoracotomy/pneumonectomy?
questions.explanation: The text lists 'FEV1 < 2 liters' as one of the operative criteria for thoracotomy/pneumonectomy.
questions.reference: Ch 19, Section: Operative Criteria for Thoracotomy/Pneumonectomy, Page 195
